name: Al-Bayt al-Mamur
description: Al-Bayt al-Mamur, meaning 'The Frequented House', is a sacred structure in Islamic cosmology, located directly above the Kaaba in the seventh heaven. Considered a divine prototype of the Kaaba, it is a place of worship for angels, who circumambulate it constantly. According to Islamic tradition, seventy thousand angels enter Al-Bayt al-Mamur each day to worship, and they never return, implying a continuous influx of new angels. Its existence highlights the interconnectedness of the earthly and heavenly realms in Islamic belief and underscores the importance of the Kaaba as a focal point of worship for both humans and celestial beings. The structure symbolizes divine presence and reinforces the concept of a universal, unified worship of God across all creation. It is a testament to the vastness and intricate organization of the cosmos as envisioned in Islamic scripture and theological discourse, often mentioned in conjunction with descriptions of the celestial realms and the divine throne.
subtitle: The Frequented House in Heaven

keyMyths.title: The Night Journey (Isra and Mi'raj)
keyMyths.description: During the Prophet Muhammad's Night Journey, he ascended to the heavens and is believed to have witnessed Al-Bayt al-Mamur. This encounter reinforced its significance as a divine sanctuary and a place of angelic devotion.

keyMyths.title: Angelic Worship
keyMyths.description: Every day, seventy thousand angels enter Al-Bayt al-Mamur to worship, and they never return. This continuous cycle highlights the ceaseless devotion of the angels and the eternal nature of divine worship.

sources.author: Muhammad ibn Jarir al-Tabari
sources.description: A comprehensive commentary on the Quran, providing detailed interpretations of verses related to the heavens and angels.
sources.text: A comprehensive commentary on the Quran, providing detailed interpretations of verses related to the heavens and angels.

sources.author: Ibn Kathir
sources.description: Another prominent Quranic commentary that elaborates on the significance of Al-Bayt al-Mamur in Islamic cosmology.
